title: Introduction
description: NOC 62201 – Funeral directors and embalmers | GTR Immigration Professional bereavement specialists under NOC 62201 are the compassionate foundation of Canada’s funeral service industry. This occupation combines technical mastery in restorative work with high-level managerial skills to support families during their most difficult moments. As a TEER 2 occupation, funeral directors and embalmers are highly valued for their specialized training. For immigration, this role offers unique pathways through the Federal Skilled Worker program and various Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs), particularly as Canada’s aging population increases the demand for professional end-of-life services.
duties: Consult with the family of the deceased regarding funeral service nature, disposition of remains, and costs.
Arrange for the transfer of remains from the place of death to the funeral home.
Inform survivors of benefits for which they may be eligible and issue death notices.
Oversee the preparation of remains, plan and schedule funeral services, and coordinate burials/cremations.
Discuss and negotiate prearranged funerals with clients.
Manage funeral home operations, including staff direction, financial records, and inventory.
Preserve, sanitize, and prepare human remains for funeral services.
Perform cosmetic and restorative work on human remains to facilitate public visitation.
Supervise funeral home attendants and other support staff during preparation processes.
employment_requirements: Funeral Directors: Completion of a one- to two-year college program and a 12- to 20-month practical apprenticeship program is usually required.
Embalmers: Completion of a two- to three-year college program and a corresponding practical apprenticeship program is mandatory.
Licensure: Licensure is required in all provinces for embalmers and in most provinces/territories for funeral directors.
Funeral directors may be required to be licensed embalmers depending on specific provincial regulations.
job_outlook: The outlook is Moderate to Good. According to the Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS), the industry will see a consistent need for new professionals as current practitioners retire. Growth is also expected as funeral homes expand their service offerings to include more personalized and diverse bereavement options.
labour_market_demand: The demand for funeral directors and embalmers is projected to remain steady across Canada, driven by an aging demographic. While it is a relatively small and specialized occupation, job openings are frequently created by the retirement of senior professionals. The industry is currently modernizing, with a growing need for directors who can manage digital memorials and diverse cultural funeral traditions.
Stable Professional Environment: Work in a regulated industry with high standards of professional ethics and practice.
Essential Service Status: Enjoy long-term job security in a sector that is not subject to typical economic fluctuations.
Meaningful Career: Provide vital emotional and logistical support to community members.
Entrepreneurial Opportunities: Many funeral directors in Canada eventually move into senior management or funeral home ownership.
pathways: Express Entry (Federal Skilled Worker): As a TEER 2 occupation, this NOC is fully eligible for the FSW stream.
Provincial Nominee Programs (PNP): Many provinces, including Ontario and BC, have specific pathways for specialized service professionals.
CEC (Canadian Experience Class): The primary route for international professionals who have completed their apprenticeship or one year of work in Canada.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): Ideal for candidates with job offers in Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, or PEI.
